data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Systems collapse triggered by earthquake storms destroying regional storage and redistribution infrastructure", "mechanism": "Tectonic clustering in the North Anatolian Fault and Aegean arc generated sequential seismic events (c.1225-1175 BC) that shattered palatial centers' administrative buildings, collapsed storerooms, and severed the centralized redistribution networks upon which Mycenaean, Hittite, and Ugaritic political economies depended; Egypt survived because its grain-storage was decentralized across the Nile valley and its political economy relied on temple-based rather than palace-based redistribution", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper", "Mesopotamian interior", "Iranian plateau"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows no seismic destruction layers at major palatial sites contemporaneously dated to collapse horizon", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite capital moved successfully after earthquake without political fragmentation", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian New Kingdom palatial economy was centrally redistributive in same manner as Hittite/Mycenaean systems", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Amos Nur's earthquake storm hypothesis; distinguishes palace-based vs temple-based redistribution as structural vulnerability"}
